walkthrough.title: Gather the Components
walkthrough.text: Let's get No Thanks set up! Grab the box and pull out the two types of components. You will find thirty-three numbered cards, each showing a number from three to thirty-five. You will also find fifty-five small round chips, sometimes called tokens. That is the entire game — beautifully simple.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle and Remove Cards
walkthrough.text: Shuffle all thirty-three cards face down so nobody can see any numbers. Now remove nine cards at random from the deck, still face down, and put them back in the box without looking at them. This is crucial — nobody knows which nine cards are missing, and that mystery is a huge part of the game.

walkthrough.title: Place the Draw Deck
walkthrough.text: Take the remaining twenty-four cards and place them as a face-down deck in the center of the table. This is the draw pile that everyone will flip from during the game. Make sure everyone can reach it comfortably.

walkthrough.title: Deal Chips to Players
walkthrough.text: Now deal out eleven chips to each player. Everyone should keep their chips hidden behind cupped hands or under the table edge — the exact number of chips each player has is secret information. Put any leftover chips back in the box. With three players you use thirty-three chips, with four players you use forty-four, and with five or more you use all fifty-five.

walkthrough.title: Choose a Starting Player
walkthrough.text: Pick someone to go first — youngest player, last person who said "no thanks" in real life, or just pick randomly. Play will go clockwise from the starting player. Flip the top card of the deck face up and you are ready to play!

summary.title: Gather the Components
bullets: 33 numbered cards (values 3–35)
55 chips (tokens)

summary.title: Shuffle and Remove Cards
bullets: Shuffle all 33 cards face down
Remove 9 cards face-down to the box — do NOT look at them
Nobody knows which 9 are missing

summary.title: Place the Draw Deck
bullets: Place remaining 24 cards as face-down deck in center
This is the shared draw pile

summary.title: Deal Chips to Players
bullets: Deal 11 chips to each player — keep chip count SECRET
3 players = 33 chips, 4 = 44, 5+ = all 55
Return extras to box

summary.title: Choose a Starting Player
bullets: Pick a starting player randomly
Play proceeds clockwise
Flip the top card face up to begin

walkthrough.title: Goal of the Game
walkthrough.text: In No Thanks, you want the lowest score possible. Cards are bad — each card adds its face value to your score. Chips are good — each chip subtracts one point from your score. The whole game is about dodging high-value cards while scooping up chips whenever you can.

walkthrough.title: Your Two Choices
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you have exactly two options. You can take the face-up card along with all the chips sitting on it. Or you can say "no thanks" and place one of your chips onto the card to pass it to the next player. That is it — take or pass. Simple as that.

walkthrough.title: Taking a Card
walkthrough.text: When you take a card, you collect the card and every chip that has piled up on it. The card goes face up in front of you for everyone to see. The chips go into your secret stash. Then you flip the next card from the deck and decide again — take it or pass. The player who takes a card always faces the next card first.

walkthrough.title: Passing (Saying No Thanks)
walkthrough.text: When you pass, you place exactly one chip from your supply onto the card. Then play moves to the next player clockwise, who faces the same choice with the same card — except now there is one more chip sweetening the deal. Cards can accumulate a big pile of chips as everyone keeps passing.

walkthrough.title: Running Out of Chips
walkthrough.text: Here is the catch — if you have zero chips, you cannot pass. You must take the current card whether you like it or not. This is why chip management matters so much. Running dry at the wrong moment can stick you with a devastating high card.

walkthrough.title: Building Runs
walkthrough.text: This is where the magic happens. If you collect consecutive numbered cards, they form a run, and only the lowest card in the run counts toward your score. For example, if you have the twenty, twenty-one, and twenty-two, you only score twenty points instead of sixty-three. Runs make expensive cards almost free if they connect to cards you already own.

walkthrough.title: Game End and Scoring
walkthrough.text: The game ends when all twenty-four cards have been taken. Now everyone scores. Add up the face values of your cards, counting each run as only its lowest card. Then subtract one point for each chip you have. Lowest total score wins! In case of a tie, the tied player with the fewest cards wins.

summary.title: Goal of the Game
bullets: Lowest score wins
Cards = penalty points (face value), chips = -1 point each

bullets: TAKE the card + all chips on it
PASS — place 1 chip on the card, next player decides

Chips accumulate on the card, sweetening the deal

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Your First Game
walkthrough.text: We are going to walk through a practice round with four players so you can see how the decisions play out. Even if you have a different player count at your table, follow along — the concepts are the same. Everyone has eleven chips hidden in their hands. Let's flip the first card!

walkthrough.title: The First Card: A Low One
walkthrough.text: The first card flipped is a seven. That is pretty low — only seven penalty points. Player one looks at it and thinks about passing, but wait. Spending a chip to avoid seven points is not a great trade. Each chip is worth one point, so you are spending one to avoid seven. Player one takes the seven and collects zero chips since it is the first flip. Smart move — low cards are cheap and not worth burning chips over.

walkthrough.title: A High Card Appears
walkthrough.text: Player one flips the next card — it is a thirty-three! That is a huge penalty. Player one definitely does not want that, so they place a chip on it and pass. Player two also passes, adding another chip. Player three passes too. Now there are three chips sitting on the thirty-three. Player four thinks about it — thirty-three points is brutal, but three chips helps offset it. Player four passes as well, and it comes back to Player one.

walkthrough.title: When Chips Pile Up
walkthrough.text: The thirty-three now has four chips on it after everyone passed once more. Player two decides to take it — thirty-three penalty points minus four chips means a net cost of twenty-nine. Not great, but Player two has a plan. If the thirty-two or thirty-four shows up later, they can build a run and only count the lower card. Plus they got four chips back to use later. Sometimes taking a bad card with lots of chips is the right play.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Burning Chips on Low Cards
walkthrough.text: A nine gets flipped. Player three places a chip to pass. Then another chip. And another. Player three just spent three chips to avoid a nine-point card. That is a terrible deal — they spent three points to dodge nine, saving only six points while draining their chip supply. Meanwhile, a thirty-point card could show up next and Player three will have fewer chips to dodge it.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Do the Math
walkthrough.text: Here is the key insight — every chip you spend is worth one point you could have subtracted from your score at the end. If you spend three chips avoiding a nine, you only saved six net points and you have three fewer chips for defense later. A good rule of thumb is to take low cards quickly, especially if there are chips on them. Save your chips for the big scary cards in the twenties and thirties.

walkthrough.title: Building a Run
walkthrough.text: A twelve gets flipped. Player four already has the eleven and the thirteen from earlier rounds. Taking the twelve would complete a run of eleven through thirteen — and the whole run would only count as eleven points instead of thirty-six. Player four snatches the twelve immediately, plus the two chips sitting on it. That is a fifteen-point swing with a bonus chip refill. Runs are the secret weapon of No Thanks.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Ignoring Run Potential
walkthrough.text: Player one has the twenty-five. A twenty-six comes up with one chip on it. Player one passes, spending a chip. The twenty-six goes around the table and Player three takes it. Now Player one has lost the chance to extend their run for just twenty-five points total instead of adding a separate card later. Worse, they spent a chip doing it.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Check for Runs
walkthrough.text: Before you decide to pass on any card, check if it is one above or one below a card you already have. A card that extends your run costs you zero extra points and often comes with bonus chips. Getting tunnel vision on the card's face value and forgetting to check your runs is one of the most common beginner mistakes. Always scan your cards before passing.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Going Broke at the Wrong Time
walkthrough.text: It is late in the game. Player three has been passing aggressively and is now down to zero chips. A thirty-one gets flipped. Player three has no choice — they must take it because they cannot pass without chips. Thirty-one penalty points, and they got zero chips from it because it was just flipped. If Player three had saved even two chips, they could have passed and let someone else deal with it.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Never Go Completely Broke
walkthrough.text: Running out of chips is the worst thing that can happen in No Thanks. You lose all power to say no, and you are at the mercy of whatever card gets flipped. Try to keep at least two or three chips in reserve at all times. Take cards with chips on them to refill your supply, even if the card is not ideal. Being broke when a thirty-five hits the table is a disaster.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring Example
walkthrough.text: Let's score Player two's hand as an example. They have the thirty-three, thirty-two, and thirty-one — that is a run, so it only counts as thirty-one. They also have the eight and the fifteen as standalone cards. Card total: thirty-one plus eight plus fifteen equals fifty-four. They have seven chips, so subtract seven. Final score: forty-seven. Not bad for someone who took a thirty-three!

walkthrough.title: You Are Ready to Play
walkthrough.text:
Congratulations — you have seen all the key decisions in No Thanks! Remember the three big lessons: take low cards quickly instead of wasting chips, always check for run connections before passing, and never let yourself go completely broke. The nine missing cards keep things unpredictable, so stay flexible and watch what other players collect.

Before you start your first real game, check out General Tips for more strategy and the Appendix for quick reference. And remember, you can always tap the Q&A tab to ask your personal GM Guide anything during the game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: The Chip Economy
walkthrough.text: Chips are your most precious resource in No Thanks. Every chip is worth exactly one point at the end of the game, but their real value is the power to say no. Think of chips as both currency and insurance. Spend them wisely on high-value cards and hoard them when the stakes are low. A player with ten chips has ten chances to dodge disaster, while a player with two chips is playing with fire.

walkthrough.title: Take Low Cards Early
walkthrough.text: Cards in the three to ten range are relatively harmless. Do not waste multiple chips avoiding them — the math does not work out. If a five gets flipped and you spend two chips passing, you only saved three net points while losing two chips of future protection. Take low cards with minimal fuss, especially if they already have chips on them. Your chips are much better spent dodging a twenty-eight or thirty-three.

walkthrough.title: Run Hunting Is Everything
walkthrough.text: Runs are the single most powerful mechanic in the game. A run of thirty, thirty-one, thirty-two, and thirty-three only costs you thirty points instead of one hundred twenty-six. That is a ninety-six point savings. Always keep your eyes open for cards that are one above or one below cards you already own. Taking a card that extends a run is almost always correct, even without any chips on it.

walkthrough.title: Watch Other Players' Cards
walkthrough.text: Everyone's cards are face up on the table. Pay attention to what other players have collected. If someone has the fifteen and seventeen, they desperately want the sixteen — which means they will probably take it before it gets to you, and you can safely pass. If a card does not help anyone's runs, it will circulate the table longer and accumulate more chips. Use this information to time your takes.

walkthrough.title: Count Chips (Roughly)
walkthrough.text: You do not need to count every chip perfectly, but keeping a rough sense of who is running low is incredibly valuable. A player with very few chips is about to be forced into taking cards, which means you can safely pass knowing they will absorb the hit. If you notice someone across from you never seems to pass, they are probably chip-rich and you should be more cautious about letting expensive cards reach them with big chip piles.

walkthrough.title: The Missing Nine Cards
walkthrough.text: Nine cards are removed at the start and nobody knows which ones. This means you can never count on a specific card appearing to complete your run. If you have the twenty and twenty-two, the twenty-one might be sitting in the box. Do not over-invest in a run that depends on a single missing card. Stay flexible and be ready to pivot if your hoped-for card never shows up.

walkthrough.title: Timing Your Takes
walkthrough.text: The best time to take a card is when it has accumulated a big pile of chips. Taking a twenty with six chips on it means a net cost of only fourteen points, and you get a massive chip refill for future defense. Be patient and let cards circulate when you can. But do not get greedy — if you wait too long, someone else might grab those chips first.

walkthrough.title: Seating Position Matters
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to who sits to your left and right. If the player to your right keeps passing on cards, those cards come to you with extra chips — great for scooping deals. If the player to your left is chip-rich, they can keep passing cards away from you after you pass. In a tighter game, knowing your neighbors' chip situations helps you predict which cards will reach you and how loaded they will be.

summary.title: The Chip Economy
bullets: Chips = -1 point each AND power to say no
Spend on high cards, conserve on low cards
10 chips = 10 chances to dodge disaster

walkthrough.title: Strategic Run Building
walkthrough.text: Advanced players do not just stumble into runs — they plan them. Look at the card distribution: numbers three through thirty-five with nine removed. That means roughly twenty-seven percent of cards are missing. When you take your first big card, immediately start tracking which adjacent cards could appear. If you take a twenty-five, the twenty-four and twenty-six become high-priority targets. Take those adjacent cards quickly, even without chips, because the run savings dwarf any chip cost.

walkthrough.title: Chip Denial
walkthrough.text: Here is a sneaky advanced tactic: let cards accumulate chips, then watch who is eyeing them. If a card with five chips on it would complete an opponent's run, consider taking it yourself — even if it does not help your runs. You gain five chips and deny your opponent a massive scoring swing. This is especially powerful in three or four player games where chip control matters more.

walkthrough.title: The Free Card Threshold
walkthrough.text: Every card has a break-even point where the chips on it equal its effective cost. A standalone fifteen with fifteen chips on it is effectively free — zero net points and fifteen chips gained. But a run-extending card is even better. If taking a twenty-one connects your twenty and twenty-two, the card costs zero points regardless of chips, and any chips on it are pure profit. Think in terms of net cost, not face value.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Removed Cards
walkthrough.text: As the game progresses, you can deduce which cards were removed. If the seventeen, eighteen, and nineteen have all appeared but the twenty has not and most of the deck is gone, the twenty is likely in the removed pile. Stop hoping for it. Conversely, if very few cards in the teens have appeared, those runs are still alive. Track the gaps and adjust your strategy accordingly — the endgame is all about probability.

walkthrough.title: Positional Pressure
walkthrough.text: Your seating position relative to chip-poor players is a weapon. If the player right before you is nearly broke, high cards will often land in their lap before reaching you. You can pass more aggressively knowing they are the safety net. Conversely, if you are sitting right before a chip-rich player, be careful about passing — they will happily add chips and send expensive cards right back to you.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Chip Conservation
walkthrough.text: In the last few rounds, every chip in your hand is a guaranteed minus one to your final score. Stop spending chips to avoid cards unless the card's effective cost is significantly higher than the chips you would spend. If there are only three cards left in the deck, you might as well hoard your chips and eat a small card rather than spend three chips passing and end up with the card anyway when it circles back.

walkthrough.title: Player Count Adjustments
walkthrough.text: The game plays very differently with three versus seven players. With three players, cards circulate quickly and chip management is tight — you see every card multiple times per cycle. With six or seven players, cards take longer to come around, and chip-heavy cards get snapped up fast. Adjust your passing threshold accordingly. In larger games, be more aggressive about taking chip-heavy cards before they reach a competitor. In smaller games, you can afford to be more patient.

walkthrough.title: The Bluff
walkthrough.text: Advanced players use bluffing to manipulate opponents. Pass confidently on a card you actually want, hoping others will pile chips on it before it reaches you again. Or take a card quickly to make opponents think you are building a run in that range, discouraging them from competing for adjacent cards. Since chip counts are hidden, your passing behavior is the main signal other players read. Use that to your advantage.

summary.title: Strategic Run Building
bullets: Plan runs proactively — track adjacent cards after each take
~27% of cards are missing, but adjacent cards are high priority
Take run-extending cards quickly, even without chips on them

summary.title: Chip Denial
bullets: Take chip-heavy cards to deny opponents' run completions
You gain chips while blocking a scoring swing
More powerful in 3-4 player games where chip control matters

summary.title: The Free Card Threshold
bullets: Break-even: chips on card = card's effective point cost
Run-extending cards cost 0 extra points → any chips = pure profit
Think in net cost, not face value

summary.title: Reading the Removed Cards
bullets: Track which cards have appeared to deduce the 9 removed ones
If a key card hasn't appeared late in the game → likely removed
Adjust run expectations based on remaining probabilities

summary.title: Positional Pressure
bullets: Chip-poor player before you = safety net for high cards
Chip-rich player after you = be cautious about passing
Use seating position as a strategic advantage

summary.title: Endgame Chip Conservation

Stop spending chips unless card cost far exceeds chip spend
With few cards left, hoard chips and eat small cards

summary.title: Player Count Adjustments

6-7 players: chip-heavy cards get snapped up — act fast

Chip counts are hidden — your behavior is the main signal

entries.definition: The deck contains thirty-three cards numbered three through thirty-five. Nine are removed at random during setup, leaving twenty-four cards in play. Nobody knows which nine are missing.

Fifty-five round tokens distributed eleven per player at setup. Each chip is worth negative one point at game end. Chips are also spent to pass on cards. Chip counts are kept secret from other players.
Pick up the face-up card and all chips accumulated on it. The card goes face up in front of you; chips join your hidden supply. You then flip the next card and face it first.
Place one chip from your supply onto the current face-up card and pass play to the next player clockwise. You cannot pass if you have zero chips.
If you have zero chips when it is your turn, you must take the current card. There is no way to avoid it. This is why chip management is critical.

A sequence of consecutively numbered cards in your collection. A run only counts as the lowest card's value for scoring. For example, cards twenty, twenty-one, and twenty-two score only twenty points instead of sixty-three.
Each card you own adds its face value to your score, except that cards in a run only count the lowest value. Higher face values mean bigger penalties.
At game end, subtract one point from your score for each chip you hold. Chips offset card penalties. A player with thirty card points and eight chips scores twenty-two.
The player with the lowest final score (card points minus chip deductions) wins. In case of a tie, the player with fewer cards wins.

The effective penalty of taking a card, calculated as the card's scoring impact minus the chips gained. A standalone card twenty with five chips has a net cost of fifteen. A run-extending card has a net cost of zero minus any chips gained.

The number of chips that must accumulate on a standalone card before taking it costs zero net points. Equals the card's face value. For run-extending cards, the break-even is zero since the card adds no points.

The nine cards removed during setup that nobody can see. As the game progresses, you can deduce which cards are missing by tracking which have appeared. Key for assessing whether a run can be completed.
Tracking roughly how many chips each opponent has based on their passing and taking behavior. Players low on chips will soon be forced to take cards, making it safer for you to pass.
